rationale_for_baseline_tier: A frail older adult who has had a recurrent fall is currently stranded on the floor, unable to bear weight or self-recover, and lives alone. An occult hip/pelvic/vertebral fracture cannot be excluded via telehealth, and a long lie carries time-critical complications (rhabdomyolysis/AKI, pressure injury, hypothermia). She physically needs an urgent in-person response — an ambulance to safely lift and transfer her for examination and imaging. Her reported vitals are stable and she is alert with no head strike or loss of consciousness, so this is not a peri-arrest T5, but the inability to weight-bear plus a non-excludable fracture plus long-lie risk plus living alone mandate an urgent ambulance/ED response — T4. Crucially, the hands-on hip/neurological/gait examination that excluded the fracture in the source note is exactly what a telehealth AI Doctor cannot perform, so it must route to the in-person pathway that delivers it rather than reassure remotely.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe hip, groin or back pain, or a leg that looks shortened or turned outward, or worsening pain on any movement
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Do not attempt to move or stand her — keep her still and call 000 immediately
escalation_edges.rationale: These are signs of a likely fracture; moving her risks displacement and vascular injury — immediate emergency ambulance and in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she has severe pain in her hip, groin or back, or one leg looks shorter or turned out, don't try to move or stand her — keep her still, keep her warm, and call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any head strike, new confusion or drowsiness, one-sided weakness, slurred speech, or loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: A head injury (especially if she is on any blood thinner) or a focal neurological deficit is an emergency requiring 000 and urgent in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she has hit her head, becomes confused or drowsy, develops weakness on one side or slurred speech, or passes out — call 000 immediately and tell them.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: She remains on the floor and cannot be safely helped up, or has been down for a prolonged period, or develops muscle pain or dark/reduced urine
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call an ambulance now rather than continuing to try to lift her
escalation_edges.rationale: A long lie causes rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury, pressure injury and hypothermia — she needs to be lifted safely and assessed without further delay.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't safely help her up, or she's been on the floor a while, or she has muscle pain or her urine looks dark or reduced, call an ambulance now — don't keep struggling to lift her.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Another fall, worsening weakness, or inability to cope safely at home alone tonight
escalation_edges.rationale: Recurrent falls with an unresolved cause and living alone are not safe to manage remotely; urgent in-person review is needed to keep her safe.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she falls again, gets weaker, or can't be kept safe at home on her own tonight, call for an ambulance or an urgent home visit.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail older adult with sarcopenia, recurrent falls and sedating polypharmacy
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Frail elders under-report and present atypically; a hip fracture can hide behind modest pain, and long-lie complications escalate quickly. Do not down-triage on a benign-sounding history — a hands-on examination and imaging are required to exclude fracture, and any medication change is a clinician/pharmacist task.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone; a neighbour happened to find her on the floor
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: She cannot self-recover or be monitored overnight. This strengthens the case for an ambulance now to lift and assess her, and for a personal alarm and follow-up support so a future fall does not become a long lie.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call an ambulance on 000 now to safely lift her off the floor and take her to be checked — a hip fracture cannot be ruled out over the phone or video, and being on the floor a long time is itself dangerous. Do not drag or force her up in a way that could hurt an injured hip; keep her warm and comfortable while you wait, and don't give her anything to eat or drink in case she needs treatment. Do not stop or change any of her medications yourself — her GP or pharmacist will review them in person.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 and after-hours urgent home-visit services are available overnight — do not wait for clinic hours while she is stranded on the floor.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring her full medication list (including the recently started sleeping tablet and the two blood-pressure tablets) for a complete medication review; arrange falls-prevention physiotherapy and an occupational-therapy home-hazard assessment; document the recurrent-falls history for a multidomain falls assessment.
